local_names.thai: ทอดมันปลา (4 ชิ้น)

food_subgroup: Processed fish products — fried fish cakes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fish cake food product class; no Thai-specific tod mun pla entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers fish cake as a processed fish product category; no Thai-specific entry. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Thai fish cake or tod mun pla identified in the clinical terminology.

main_ingredients: fish (typically snakehead fish or white fish)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Thai fish cake consists of 4 pieces, weighing approximately 120 g in total, typically served as a starter or side dish with sweet chilli dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fish cake, fried — Nutrient composition per 100 g
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central, Foundation Foods / SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA entry for Thai tod mun pla exists. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for fish cake (fried, mixed fish) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian fried fish products. Thai fish cakes typically contain red curry paste, kaffir lime leaves, and long beans in addition to fish, which slightly elevates carbohydrate and sodium content relative to plain fried fish cake. Values represent a best-estimate composite for a Thai-style fried fish cake at 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fish and seafood products, fried fish cake analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Thai tod mun pla (fish cake) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 (low category) based on analogous fried fish cake and mixed fish patty entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). Fish-based fried products with moderate carbohydrate content from starch binders and curry paste typically fall in the GI range 35–45. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 12.24) / 100 ≈ 4.9, where carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 10.2 × 1.2 = 12.24 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~624 mg per 4-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Thai fish cake intake due to its high sodium content from fish sauce and curry paste; a single 4-piece serving provides approximately 27% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (~10 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai fish cake has a low glycemic index of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of approximately 4.9 per 4-piece serving, making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.4 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~520 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Thai fish cake provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish but also contains moderate saturated fat and high sodium from fish sauce and curry paste; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume it in moderation and avoid high-sodium accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai fish cake (tod mun pla, ทอดมันปลา) is a traditional Thai appetiser or side dish made from minced white fish or snakehead fish blended with red curry paste, kaffir lime leaves, finely sliced long beans, egg, fish sauce, and a small amount of sugar. The mixture is shaped into small round patties and deep-fried or pan-fried until golden brown and slightly crispy on the outside, while remaining springy and moist on the inside. Each piece is typically 25–35 g, and a standard serving consists of 4 pieces (approximately 120 g). The dish is characterised by a savoury, mildly spicy flavour with aromatic notes from kaffir lime and lemongrass, and a firm yet bouncy texture distinctive of fish-based products. Tod mun pla is commonly served with a sweet chilli cucumber relish (ajad). Nutritionally, Thai fish cake is a moderate-energy food providing approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with a good protein content of approximately 14.5 g per 100 g, making it a meaningful protein source. Fat content is approximately 9.8 g per 100 g, with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio due to the fish base. Carbohydrate content is moderate at approximately 10.2 g per 100 g, contributed by starch binders and curry paste ingredients. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 520 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce and curry paste, which is a key clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low category), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar sauces. The dish is dairy-free and rich in B vitamins, particularly B12, from the fish component.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai fish cake?
Is fish cake good for diabetics?
How much sodium in Thai fish cake?
What is in Thai fish cake?
Is Thai fish cake gluten free?
Thai fish cake nutrition per piece

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai tod mun pla was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried fish cake data cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Southeast Asian fried fish products. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and the food's composition is well-characterised by its known ingredients.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Desserts' (as in input seed record) to 'Fish and seafood', which is the correct classification for Thai fish cake. The input food_group assignment appears to be a data-entry error in the field survey seed record. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB/USDA exact match for Thai-style tod mun pla would improve confidence to high. 3. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA SR Legacy source for fried fish cake. 4. Halal status is set to false as the restaurant (Sanook Kitchen) is not confirmed halal-certified; fish sauce may contain non-halal additives. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tod mun pla was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the adapted USDA SR Legacy source and FAO/INFOODS SEA data; these are best-estimate values and not confirmed by direct measurement. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source for fried fish cake.
